title: Nordic klipp, folded symmetry papercutting
subtitle: How to produce eight-fold and four-fold symmetrical papercut designs
excerpt: Nordic klipp (from the Swedish and Norwegian word for cut) is a tradition of folding a square of paper into quarters or eighths and cutting decorative shapes through all layers simultaneously. When unfolded, the cut reveals a perfectly symmetrical snowflake or rosette. The technique requires only paper and scissors.
sourceNotes: Folded symmetry papercutting has been documented in Scandinavian folk craft since at least the early 19th century. The klippekunst tradition appears in Norwegian and Swedish folk-art surveys from the 1860s onward, describing the technique as a domestic craft taught from childhood.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Scandinavian word for 'cut'; used to describe the tradition of folded-and-cut symmetry paperwork in Norwegian and Swedish folk craft. The resulting pieces are often called klippkunst or papirklipp.

A papercut folded into eighths before cutting, which produces eight identical repeated sections when unfolded. A square folded in half, then in half again, then diagonally once gives eight layers and eight-fold symmetry.

content.text: is done with scissors rather than a knife. The folded paper is held in one hand and turned while cutting freehand with the other. The approach described here draws a guide pattern before cutting, which is useful for a first attempt.
is the most rewarding to start with: even a simple cut produces an intricate-looking result when unfolded.

attrs.title: Knife and scissors safety
attrs.body: If using a craft knife rather than scissors for interior cuts, always cut away from fingers and use a metal ruler for straight cuts. Keep the blade sharp. Replace blades when they begin to drag rather than cut cleanly.

items.name: Paper square, 80-100 gsm
items.notes: Any size from 10 cm square upward. Thin paper cuts more cleanly through eight layers; thicker paper is harder to fold precisely and resists the scissors.

items.name: Pencil
items.notes: For drawing the cut guide on the folded paper before cutting.

items.name: Bone folder
items.notes: For pressing each fold flat before making the next fold. Sharp, clean folds are critical for accurate symmetry.

items.name: Craft knife and cutting mat (optional)
items.notes: Only needed for interior cuts that scissors cannot reach. Most klipp designs can be cut entirely with scissors.

content.text: Start with a paper square. Fold it in half vertically, pressing the fold flat with the bone folder. Fold in half again horizontally. You now have a quarter-square with four layers. Fold this diagonally from corner to corner, keeping the two folded edges together. Press flat. You now have eight layers and the folded block is a triangle. Cuts made into this triangle will produce

Draw a simple design on the folded triangle in pencil. Avoid cutting along any of the folded edges, leaving the fold edges intact in at least two places holds the piece together when unfolded. Interior shapes can be removed entirely; the cut-away areas become the open decorative elements. Cut firmly through all eight layers in a single pass; do not saw back and forth, which tears the edges.

carefully, opening each fold layer by layer. Lay it face-down on a clean surface and press flat under a heavy book for 30 minutes. Mount on a contrasting background sheet for display, or use as a decorative element in a journal page or card.
